claim: Model distillation involves training smaller/weaker models on outputs of stronger models; can be done on outputs only or on logits (probability distributions) for finer-grained learning
actionable: When building smaller models, consider distillation on model outputs or logits; this is lower-cost than training from scratch while preserving capabilities

claim: Distillation is commonly used by frontier labs to create smaller inference-optimized versions (flash, haiku models) from larger base models; may also apply to largest models despite public claims
actionable: Assume frontier model 'families' (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku) likely involve distillation; smaller models aren't independently trained but knowledge-transferred variants

claim: Industrial-scale API abuse via fake accounts can extract reasoning abilities, tool use, and coding skills at scale; detection requires pattern analysis of query behavior
actionable: If operating a high-value model API, implement behavioral analysis to detect coordinated extraction attempts (similar prompts, fake account clustering)

claim: Training on outputs of proprietary models is legal/common practice in ML (GPT-4 distillation), but frontier labs now treat it as security threat when targeting their models specifically
actionable: Be aware that distillation from model outputs is gray area; frontier labs may claim IP violation even when legal, creating business/reputational risk

claim: Using LLM outputs as reward models for reinforcement learning (rubric-based grading) is effective distillation technique because LLM-as-judge encodes judgment implicitly in training
actionable: For fine-tuning with distillation, use strong model as reward model to grade your model's outputs; iterate to maximize agreement with judge model
